narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because a model flagged a 47% chance of not passing the bar exam feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why it landed there, like piecing together a story from the clues in your background. The biggest chapter in this tale is your Year 3 decile of 1, which put you at the very bottom of your class that year and screamed the loudest warning to the model about potential struggles ahead on the bar. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 6, still middling at best and reinforcing a pattern of grades that didn't climb as high as they needed to for confidence in your exam readiness. Your LSAT score of 26.5 added another layer, pulling firmly toward the risk of failure since it falls short of the benchmarks that typically light the path to success. Even your middle-family income bracket played a role, hinting at fewer resources for the intense prep that bar passage often demands. Your white racial background and undergraduate GPA of 2.7 each nudged the prediction downward too, fitting into patterns the model has seen before where these don't quite offset other hurdles. Choosing part-time status while studying contributed as well, suggesting the balancing act of work and law school might dilute your focus. The one bright spot was your gender as male, which slightly tilted things toward passing in the model's view, but it wasn't enough to rewrite the overall story. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone who's capable and determined, yet facing an uphill battle against odds stacked by early academic signals and limited buffers. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential—it's just the model's honest read based on what similar paths have shown before. If you pour energy into boosting those grades or LSAT prep, you could flip this narrative entirely for another try. Hang in there; your story's far from over.
